From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Treatise on cartography by Claudius Ptolemaeus

Geography

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| The world map after Ptolemy's first projection from a Greek manuscript edition of the Geography (Burney MS 111, f.105v-106r) | |
| Author | [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") |
| Original title | *Γεωγραφικὴ Ὑφήγησις* |
| Language | [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") |
| Genre | [Geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography") |
| Publication date | c. 150s A.D. |
| Media type | [Manuscript](/wiki/Manuscript "Manuscript") |
| Preceded by | *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* |
| Followed by | *[Table of Noteworthy Cities](/wiki/Table_of_Noteworthy_Cities_(Ptolemy) "Table of Noteworthy Cities (Ptolemy)")* |

The ***Geography*** ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Γεωγραφικὴ Ὑφήγησις, *Geōgraphikḕ Hyphḗgēsis*, lit. "Geographical Guidance"), also known by its [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") names as the ****Geographia**** and the ****Cosmographia****, is a [gazetteer](/wiki/Gazetteer "Gazetteer"), an [atlas](/wiki/Atlas_(book) "Atlas (book)"), and a treatise on [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography"), compiling the geographical knowledge of the 2nd-century [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). Originally written by [Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Claudius_Ptolemy "Claudius Ptolemy") in [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") around 150 AD, the work was a revision of a now-lost atlas by [Marinus of Tyre](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre") using additional Roman and [Persian](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire") gazetteers and new principles.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerggren2000-1)

Its translation into [Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic") by [al-Khwarismi](/wiki/Al-Khwarizmi "Al-Khwarizmi") in the 9th century was highly influential on the [geographical knowledge and cartographic traditions of the Islamic world](/wiki/Geography_and_cartography_in_medieval_Islam "Geography and cartography in medieval Islam"). Alongside the works of Islamic scholars—and the commentary containing revised and more accurate data by [Alfraganus](/wiki/Alfraganus "Alfraganus")—Ptolemy's work was subsequently highly influential on [Medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") Europe.

## Manuscripts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Manuscripts")]

Versions of Ptolemy's work in antiquity were probably proper [atlases](/wiki/Atlas_(book) "Atlas (book)") with attached maps, although some scholars believe that the references to maps in the text were later additions.

No [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") manuscript of the *Geography* survives from earlier than the 13th century.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDilke1987b267–268-2) However fragmentary [papyri](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus") of later somewhat derivative works such as the [Table of Noteworthy Cities](/wiki/Table_of_Noteworthy_Cities_(Ptolemy) "Table of Noteworthy Cities (Ptolemy)") have been found with the earliest, [Rylands Library](/wiki/John_Rylands_Research_Institute_and_Library "John Rylands Research Institute and Library") [GP 522](https://luna.manchester.ac.uk/luna/servlet/detail/ManchesterDev~93~3~55169~189080), dating to the early 3rd century.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) A letter written by the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") [monk](/wiki/Monk "Monk") [Maximus Planudes](/wiki/Maximus_Planudes "Maximus Planudes") records that he searched for one for [Chora Monastery](/wiki/Chora_Monastery "Chora Monastery") in the summer of 1295;[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDilke1987b268-5) one of the earliest surviving texts may have been one of those he then assembled.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) In Europe, maps were sometimes redrawn using the coordinates provided by the text,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilanesi1996-8) as Planudes was forced to do.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDilke1987b268-5) Later scribes and publishers could then copy these new maps, as [Athanasius](/wiki/Athanasius_I_of_Constantinople "Athanasius I of Constantinople") did for the [emperor](/wiki/List_of_Byzantine_emperors "List of Byzantine emperors") [Andronicus II Palaeologus](/wiki/Andronicus_II_Palaeologus "Andronicus II Palaeologus").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDilke1987b268-5) The three earliest surviving texts with maps are those from [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") ([Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul")) based on Planudes's work.[[a]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Rylands_Library_Greek_Papyrus_522.jpg/250px-Rylands_Library_Greek_Papyrus_522.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rylands_Library_Greek_Papyrus_522.jpg)

A 3rd century [papyrus](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus") fragment of Ptolemy's [Table of Noteworthy Cities](/wiki/Table_of_Noteworthy_Cities_(Ptolemy) "Table of Noteworthy Cities (Ptolemy)")

The first [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") translation of these texts was made in 1406 or 1407 by [Jacobus Angelus](/wiki/Jacopo_d%27Angelo "Jacopo d'Angelo") in [Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), under the name **Geographia Claudii Ptolemaei**.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAngelusc._1406-16) It is not thought that his edition had maps,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClemens2008244-17) although [Manuel Chrysoloras](/wiki/Manuel_Chrysoloras "Manuel Chrysoloras") had given [Palla Strozzi](/wiki/Palla_Strozzi "Palla Strozzi") a Greek copy of Planudes's maps in Florence in 1397.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

### Stemma

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Stemma")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Ptolemy_Geography_Hyphegesis_Stemma_Codicum.jpg/250px-Ptolemy_Geography_Hyphegesis_Stemma_Codicum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ptolemy_Geography_Hyphegesis_Stemma_Codicum.jpg)

[Stemma codicum](/wiki/Textual_criticism "Textual criticism") of the *Geography*.

Berggren & Jones (2000) place these manuscripts into a [stemma](/wiki/Textual_criticism#Stemmatics "Textual criticism") whereby U, K, F and N are connected with the activities of [Maximos Planudes](/wiki/Maximus_Planudes "Maximus Planudes") (c.1255-1305). From a sister manuscript to UKFN descends R, V, W & C, however the maps were either copied defectively or not at all. "Of the greatest importance for the text of the Geography" they state is manuscript X ([Vat.Gr.191](https://digi.vatlib.it/mss/detail/Vat.gr.191)); "because it is the only copy that is uninfluenced by the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") revision." e.g. the 13th-14th century corrections of Planudes, possibly associated with recreating the maps.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-19)

Regarding the maps, they conclude that it was unlikely that extant maps survived from which the above stemma descends, even if maps existed in antiquity:

> "The transmission of [Ptolemy's](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") text certainly passed through a stage when the manuscripts were too small to contain the maps. Planudes and his assistants therefore probably had no pictorial models, and the success of their enterprise is proof that Ptolemy succeeded in his attempt to encode the map in words and numbers. The copies of the maps in later manuscripts and printed editions of the Geography were reproduced from Planudes' reconstructions."[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Mittenhuber (2010) further divides the stemma into two recensions of the original c.AD 150 lost work: [Ξ](/wiki/Xi_(letter) "Xi (letter)") and [Ω](/wiki/Omega "Omega") (c.3rd/4th cent., lost).[[22]](#cite_note-:1-23) Recension Ω contains most of the extant manuscripts and is subdivided into a further two groups: [Δ](/wiki/Delta_(letter) "Delta (letter)") and [Π](/wiki/Pi_(letter) "Pi (letter)"). Group Δ contains parchment manuscripts from the end of the thirteenth century, which are the earliest extant manuscripts of the Geography; these are U, K & F. Recension, Ξ, is represented by one codex only, X. Mittenhuber agrees with Berggren & Jones, stating that "The so-called Codex X is of particular significance, because it contains many local names and coordinates that differ from the other manuscripts ... which cannot be explained by mere errors in the tradition.".
Although no manuscripts survive from earlier than the late 13th century; there are references to the existence of ancient [codicies](/wiki/Codex "Codex") in late antiquity. One such example is in an epistle by [Cassiodorus](/wiki/Cassiodorus "Cassiodorus") (c.560 A.D.):

> “Tum, si vos notitiae nobilis cura inflammaverit, habetis [Ptolemaei](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") codicem, qui sic omnia loca evidenter expressit, ut eum cunctarum regionum paene incolam fuisse iudicetis. Eoque fit, ut uno loco positi, sicut monachos decet, animo percurratis, quod aliquorum peregrinatio plurimo labore collegit.”([Institutiones](/wiki/Cassiodorus#Works "Cassiodorus") 1, 25)[[22]](#cite_note-:1-23).

The existence of ancient recensions that differ fundamentally to the surviving manuscript tradition can be seen in the epitomes of [Markianos](/wiki/Marcian_of_Heraclea "Marcian of Heraclea") by [Stephanus](/wiki/Stephanus_of_Byzantium "Stephanus of Byzantium"):

> "Καὶ ἄλλοι οὕτως διὰ του π [Πρετανίδες](/wiki/Britain_(place_name) "Britain (place name)") νῆσοι, ὡς [Μαρκιανὸς](/wiki/Marcian_of_Heraclea "Marcian of Heraclea") καὶ [Πτολεμαῖος](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")."[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

The tradition preserved within the stemma of surviving (13th-14th century) manuscripts by Stückelberger & Grasshoff only preserves "[Β](/wiki/Beta "Beta")" and not "[Π](/wiki/Pi_(letter) "Pi (letter)")" recentions of "[Βρεττανικήσ](/wiki/Britain_(place_name) "Britain (place name)")".[[27]](#cite_note-:2-28)

## Contents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Contents")]

The *Geography* consists of three sections, divided among 8 books. Book I is a treatise on [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") and [chorography](/wiki/Chorography "Chorography"), describing the methods used to assemble and arrange Ptolemy's data. From Book II through the beginning of Book VII, a gazetteer provides longitude and latitude values for the [world known](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene") to the [ancient Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans") (the "[ecumene](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene")"). The rest of Book VII provides details on three projections to be used for the construction of a map of the world, varying in complexity and fidelity. Book VIII constitutes an [atlas](/wiki/Atlas_(book) "Atlas (book)") of regional maps. The maps include a recapitulation of some of the values given earlier in the work, which were intended to be used as captions to clarify the map's contents and maintain their accuracy during copying. Book 8 formed the basis for the [Table of Noteworthy Cities](/wiki/Table_of_Noteworthy_Cities_(Ptolemy) "Table of Noteworthy Cities (Ptolemy)").

### Cartographical treatise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cartographical treatise")]

Maps based on [scientific principles](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") had been made in Europe since the time of [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") in the 3rd century BC. Ptolemy improved the treatment of [map projections](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection").[[28]](#cite_note-29) He provided instructions on how to create his maps in the first section of the work.

### Gazetteer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Gazetteer")]

The gazetteer section of Ptolemy's work provided [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate "Coordinate") for all the places and geographical features in the work. Latitude was expressed in [degrees of arc](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)") from the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), the same system that is used now, though Ptolemy used fractions of a degree rather than minutes of arc.[[29]](#cite_note-30) His [Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian"), of [0](/wiki/0 "0") [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), ran through the [Fortunate Isles](/wiki/Fortunate_Isles "Fortunate Isles"), the westernmost land recorded,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright1923-31) at around the position of [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro") in the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands").[[31]](#cite_note-32) The maps spanned 180 degrees of longitude from the Fortunate Isles in the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to [China](/wiki/China "China").

Ptolemy was aware that Europe knew only about a quarter of the globe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Atlas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Atlas")]

Ptolemy's work included a single large and less detailed world map and then separate and more detailed regional maps. The first Greek manuscripts compiled after [Maximus Planudes](/wiki/Maximus_Planudes "Maximus Planudes")'s rediscovery of the text had as many as 64 regional maps.[[b]](#cite_note-34) The standard set in Western Europe came to be 26: 10 European maps, 4 African maps, and 12 Asian maps. As early as the 1420s, these canonical maps were complemented by extra-Ptolemaic regional maps depicting, e.g., [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia").

## Chapters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Chapters")]

The Geography is spread over 8 books with the main body of the work (books 2-7) is a list of some 8000 [toponyms](/wiki/Toponymy "Toponymy") comprising the [Oikumene](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene") of the [second century AD](/wiki/2nd_century "2nd century"). Book 1 is written in prose and is Ptolemy's explanation of the project, his method and his sources (mainly [Marinos of Tyre](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre")). Book 8 offers descriptions for each of the maps created in books 2-7 and forms the basis of the [Table of Noteworthy Cities](/wiki/Table_of_Noteworthy_Cities_(Ptolemy) "Table of Noteworthy Cities (Ptolemy)"). The [critical edition](/wiki/Textual_criticism "Textual criticism") was published by Stückelberger, Mittenhuber and Klöti (2006).[[27]](#cite_note-:2-28)

### Book 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Book 1")]

Book 1 is a theoretical treatise by Ptolemy outlining the subject matter, previous work and instructing the reader how to draw a world map using his projection systems. The sections are, to use Ptolemy's original titles:[[33]](#cite_note-35)

1. On the difference between [world cartography](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") and regional [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography")
2. On the prerequisites for world cartography
3. How the number of [stades](/wiki/Stadion_(unit) "Stadion (unit)") in the [earth's circumference](/wiki/Earth%27s_circumference "Earth's circumference") can be [obtained](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") from the number of stades in an arbitrary rectilinear interval, and vice versa, even if [the interval] is not on a single [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)")
4. That it is necessary to give priority to the [[astronomical](/wiki/Celestial_cartography "Celestial cartography")] phenomena over [data] from records of travel
5. That it is necessary to follow the most recent researches because of changes in the world over time
6. On [Marinos](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre")' guide to world cartography
7. Revision of Marinos' [latitudinal](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") dimension of the known world on the basis of the [astronomical] phenomena
8. The same revision [of the latitudinal dimension], on the basis of land journeys
9. The same revision [of the latitudinal dimension], on the basis of sea journeys
10. That one should not put the [Aithiopians](/wiki/Aethiopian_Sea "Aethiopian Sea") south of the parallel situated opposite to that through [Meroe](/wiki/Mero%C3%AB "Meroë")
11. On the computations that Marinos improperly made for the [longitudinal](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") dimension of the [oikoumene](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene")
12. The revision of the longitudinal dimension of the known world on the basis of journeys by land
13. The same revision [of the longitudinal dimension] on the basis of journeys by sea
14. On the crossing from the [Golden Peninsula](/wiki/Golden_Chersonese "Golden Chersonese") to [Kattigara](/wiki/Cattigara "Cattigara")
15. On the inconsistencies in details of Marinos' exposition
16. That certain matters escaped [Marinos'] notice in the boundaries of the provinces
17. On the inconsistencies between [Marinos] and the reports ofour time
18. On the inconvenience of Marinos' compilations for drawing a map of the oikoumene
19. On the convenience of our catalogue for making a map
20. On the disproportional nature of Marinos' geographical map
21. On the things that should be preserved in a [planar map](/wiki/Planar_graph "Planar graph")
22. On how one should make a map of the oikoumene on a [globe](/wiki/Globe "Globe")
23. List of the [meridians](/wiki/Meridians_of_longitude "Meridians of longitude") and [parallels](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude") to be included in the map
24. Method of making a map of the oikoumene in the plane in proper proportionality with its configuration on the globe (In this section Ptolemy explains two methods for projecting his map)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: History")]

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

The original treatise by [Marinus of Tyre](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre") that formed the basis of Ptolemy's *Geography* has been completely lost. A world map based on Ptolemy was displayed in [Augustodunum](/wiki/Augustodunum "Augustodunum") ([Autun](/wiki/Autun "Autun"), [France](/wiki/France "France")) in late Roman times.[[36]](#cite_note-38) [Pappus](/wiki/Pappus_of_Alexandria "Pappus of Alexandria"), writing at [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") in the 4th century, produced a [commentary](/wiki/Scholia "Scholia") on Ptolemy's *Geography* and used it as the basis of his (now lost) *Chorography of the Ecumene*.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDilke1987a234-39) Later imperial writers and mathematicians, however, seem to have restricted themselves to commenting on Ptolemy's text, rather than improving upon it; surviving records actually show decreasing fidelity to real position.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDilke1987a234-39) Nevertheless, Byzantine scholars continued these geographical traditions throughout the Medieval period.[[38]](#cite_note-40)

Whereas previous Greco-Roman geographers such as [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") and [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder") demonstrated a reluctance to rely on the contemporary accounts of sailors and merchants who plied distant areas of the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), Marinus and Ptolemy betray a much greater receptiveness to incorporating information received from them.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2008118-41) For instance, Grant Parker argues that it would be highly implausible for them to have constructed the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal") as precisely as they did without the accounts of sailors.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2008118-41) When it comes to the account of the [Golden Chersonese](/wiki/Golden_Chersonese "Golden Chersonese") (i.e. [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula")) and the *[Magnus Sinus](/wiki/Magnus_Sinus "Magnus Sinus")* (i.e. [Gulf of Thailand](/wiki/Gulf_of_Thailand "Gulf of Thailand") and [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea")), Marinus and Ptolemy relied on the testimony of a Greek sailor named Alexandros, who claimed to have visited a far eastern site called "[Cattigara](/wiki/Cattigara "Cattigara")" (most likely [Oc Eo](/wiki/Oc_Eo "Oc Eo"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), the site of unearthed [Antonine](/wiki/Antonine "Antonine")-era Roman goods and not far from the region of [Jiaozhi](/wiki/Jiaozhi "Jiaozhi") in northern Vietnam where [ancient Chinese sources](/wiki/Twenty-Four_Histories "Twenty-Four Histories") claim several [Roman embassies](/wiki/Sino-Roman_relations "Sino-Roman relations") first landed in the 2nd and 3rd centuries).[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung200129-42)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMawer201338-43)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESuárez199990-92-44)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYule191552-45)

### Medieval Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Medieval Islam")]

See also: [Geography and cartography in medieval Islam](/wiki/Geography_and_cartography_in_medieval_Islam "Geography and cartography in medieval Islam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Amirbliulaie.png/250px-Amirbliulaie.png)](/wiki/File:Amirbliulaie.png)

The Amir of Bani Bu Ali tribe, the likely *Bliulaie* of Ptolemy's map.

[Muslim cartographers](/wiki/Geography_and_cartography_in_medieval_Islam "Geography and cartography in medieval Islam") were using copies of Ptolemy's *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* and *Geography* by the 9th century.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdson200461–62-46) At that time, in the court of the [caliph](/wiki/List_of_caliphs "List of caliphs") [al-Maʾmūm](/wiki/Al-Ma%CA%BEm%C5%ABm "Al-Maʾmūm"), [al-Khwārazmī](/wiki/Al-Khw%C4%81razm%C4%AB "Al-Khwārazmī") compiled his *[Book of the Depiction of the Earth](/wiki/Book_of_the_Depiction_of_the_Earth "Book of the Depiction of the Earth")* (*Kitab Surat al-Ard*) which mimicked the *Geography*[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008128-47) in providing the coordinates for 545 cities and regional maps of the [Nile](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River"), the [Island of the Jewel](/wiki/Island_of_the_Jewel "Island of the Jewel"), the Sea of Darkness, and the [Sea of Azov](/wiki/Sea_of_Azov "Sea of Azov").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008128-47) A 1037 copy of these are the earliest extant maps from Islamic lands.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008127-48) The text clearly states that al-Khwārazmī was working from an earlier map, although this could not have been an exact copy of Ptolemy's work: his [Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian") was 10° east of Ptolemy's, he adds some places, and his latitudes differ.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008128-47) [C.A. Nallino](/wiki/C.A._Nallino "C.A. Nallino") suggests that the work was not based on Ptolemy but on a derivative world map,[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENallino1939-49) presumably in [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") or [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008128-47) The coloured map of al-Maʾmūm constructed by a team including al-Khwārazmī was described by the [Persian](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people") encyclopædist [al-Masʿūdī](/wiki/Al-Mas%CA%BF%C5%ABd%C4%AB "Al-Masʿūdī") around 956 as superior to the maps of [Marinus](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre") and Ptolemy,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEal-Masʿūdī189433-50) probably indicating that it was built along similar mathematical principles.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008130-51) It included 4530 cities and over 200 mountains.

Despite beginning to compile numerous gazetteers of places and coordinates indebted to Ptolemy,[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008129-52) Muslim scholars made almost no direct use of Ptolemy's principles in the maps which have survived.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdson200461–62-46) Instead, they followed al-Khwārazmī's modifications and the [orthogonal projection](/wiki/Orthographic_map_projection "Orthographic map projection") advocated by Suhrāb's early 10th-century treatise on the *Marvels of the Seven Climes to the End of Habitation*. Surviving maps from the medieval period were not done according to mathematical principles. The world map from the 11th-century *Book of Curiosities* is the earliest surviving map of the [Muslim](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate") or [Christian](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") worlds to include a [geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") but the copyist seems to have not understood its purpose, starting it from the left using twice the intended scale and then (apparently realizing his mistake) giving up halfway through.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008126–127-53) Its presence does strongly suggest the existence of earlier, now-lost maps which had been mathematically derived in the manner of Ptolemy,[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008127-48) al-Khwārazmi, or Suhrāb. There are surviving reports of such maps.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapoport2008129-52)

Ptolemy's *Geography* was translated from [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") into [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") at the court of King [Roger II of Sicily](/wiki/Roger_II_of_Sicily "Roger II of Sicily") in the 12th century AD.[[52]](#cite_note-54) However, no copy of that translation has survived.

### Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Renaissance")]

Further information: [Waldseemüller map](/wiki/Waldseem%C3%BCller_map "Waldseemüller map"), [Sino-Roman relations](/wiki/Sino-Roman_relations "Sino-Roman relations"), [Indo-Roman relations](/wiki/Indo-Roman_relations "Indo-Roman relations"), [Europeans in Medieval China](/wiki/Europeans_in_Medieval_China "Europeans in Medieval China"), and [Chronology of European exploration of Asia](/wiki/Chronology_of_European_exploration_of_Asia "Chronology of European exploration of Asia")

The Greek text of the *Geography* reached [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") from [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") in about 1400 and was translated into Latin by [Jacobus Angelus](/wiki/Jacopo_d%27Angelo "Jacopo d'Angelo") of [Scarperia](/wiki/Scarperia "Scarperia") around 1406.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAngelusc._1406-16) The reception of the *Geography* in Latin Europe was diverse. In the first half of the 15th century, [Florentine](/wiki/Florence "Florence") [humanists](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") used it mainly as a [philological](/wiki/Philology "Philology") resource to understand the geography of ancient texts; [Venetian](/wiki/Venice "Venice") cartographers attempted to reconcile Ptolemaic maps with [portolan charts](/wiki/Portolan_chart "Portolan chart") and medieval [mappaemundi](/wiki/Mappa_mundi "Mappa mundi"), and French and German scholars with an interest in [astrology](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology") focused on Ptolemy's [cosmographical](/wiki/Cosmography "Cosmography") concepts.[[53]](#cite_note-55) Over the second half of the century, the prestige of the *Geography* grew to become the necessary framework of any reflection on geographical space.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

The first printed edition with maps, published in 1477 in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), was also the first printed book with engraved illustrations.[[55]](#cite_note-Landau1996-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58) Many editions followed (more often using [woodcut](/wiki/Woodcut "Woodcut") in the early days), some following traditional versions of the maps, and others updating them.[[55]](#cite_note-Landau1996-57) An edition published at [Ulm](/wiki/Ulm "Ulm") in 1482 was the first one printed north of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"). It became a commercial success and was reprinted in 1486.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Also in 1482, [Francesco Berlinghieri](/wiki/Francesco_Berlinghieri "Francesco Berlinghieri") printed the first edition in vernacular [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"). The edition published in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") in 1513 was a major step in the modernization of the *Geography*. It preserved the corpus of Ptolemy's text and maps as faithfully as possible to the original while it provided a separate set of 20 more accurate and up-to-date modern maps.[[58]](#cite_note-60) A much improved Latin translation of the Greek original was produced by [Willibald Pirckheimer](/wiki/Willibald_Pirckheimer "Willibald Pirckheimer") for the 1525 Strasbourg edition, and the first printed edition directly in Greek was authored by [Erasmus of Rotterdam](/wiki/Erasmus "Erasmus") in [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel") in 1533.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Cosmographia%2C_1482.djvu/page7-250px-Cosmographia%2C_1482.djvu.jpg)](/w/index.php?title=File:Cosmographia,_1482.djvu&page=7)

Edition printed in [Ulm](/wiki/Ulm "Ulm") in 1482

Ptolemy had mapped the whole world from the *Fortunatae Insulae* ([Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde")[[59]](#cite_note-61) or [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands")) eastward to the eastern shore of the *Magnus Sinus*. This known portion of the world was comprised within 180 degrees. In his extreme east Ptolemy placed *Serica* (the Land of Silk), the *Sinarum Situs* (the Port of the [Sinae](/wiki/Sinae "Sinae")), and the [emporium](/wiki/Emporium_(antiquity) "Emporium (antiquity)") of *[Cattigara](/wiki/Kattigara "Kattigara")*. On the 1489 map of the world by Henricus Martellus, which was based on Ptolemy's work, Asia terminated in its southeastern point in a cape, the Cape of Cattigara. Cattigara was understood by Ptolemy to be a port on the *Sinus Magnus,* or Great Gulf, the actual Gulf of Thailand, at eight and a half degrees north of the Equator, on the coast of Cambodia, which is where he located it in his *Canon of Famous Cities*. It was the easternmost port reached by shipping trading from the Graeco-Roman world to the lands of the Far East.[[60]](#cite_note-62)
In Ptolemy's later and better-known *Geography*, a scribal error was made and Cattigara was located at eight and a half degrees South of the Equator. On Ptolemaic maps, such as that of Martellus, *Catigara* was located on the easternmost shore of the *Mare Indicum,* 180 degrees East of the Cape St Vincent at, due to the scribal error, eight and a half degrees South of the Equator.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

*Catigara* is also shown at this location on Martin Waldseemüller's 1507 world map, which avowedly followed the tradition of Ptolemy. Ptolemy's information was thereby misinterpreted so that the coast of China, which should have been represented as part of the coast of eastern Asia, was falsely made to represent an eastern shore of the Indian Ocean. As a result, Ptolemy implied more land east of the 180th meridian and an ocean beyond. [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo")’s account of his travels in eastern Asia described lands and seaports on an eastern ocean apparently unknown to Ptolemy. Marco Polo’s narrative authorized the extensive additions to the Ptolemaic map shown on the 1492 globe of [Martin Behaim](/wiki/Martin_Behaim "Martin Behaim"). The fact that Ptolemy did not represent an eastern coast of Asia made it admissible for Behaim to extend that continent far to the east. Behaim’s globe placed Marco Polo’s Mangi and [Cathay](/wiki/Cathay "Cathay") east of Ptolemy’s 180th meridian, and [the Great Khan’s](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan") capital, [*Cambaluc*](/wiki/Khanbaliq "Khanbaliq") ([Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing")), on the 41st parallel of latitude at approximately 233 degrees East. Behaim allowed 60 degrees beyond Ptolemy’s 180 degrees for the mainland of Asia and 30 degrees more to the east coast of *[Cipangu](/wiki/Cipangu "Cipangu")* (Japan). Cipangu and the mainland of Asia were thus placed only 90 and 120 degrees, respectively, west of the Canary Islands.

The Codex Seragliensis was used as the base of a new edition of the work in 2006.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStückelberger2006-14) This new edition was used to "decode" Ptolemy's coordinates of Books 2 and 3 by an interdisciplinary team of [TU Berlin](/wiki/TU_Berlin "TU Berlin"), presented in publications in 2010[[62]](#cite_note-64) and 2012.[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)

#### Influence on Christopher Columbus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Influence on Christopher Columbus")]

[Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") modified this geography further by using 53+2⁄3 Italian nautical miles as the length of a degree instead of the longer degree of Ptolemy, and by adopting [Marinus of Tyre](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre")’s longitude of 225 degrees for the east coast of the *[Magnus Sinus](/wiki/Magnus_Sinus "Magnus Sinus")*. This resulted in a considerable eastward advancement of the longitudes given by [Martin Behaim](/wiki/Martin_Behaim "Martin Behaim") and other contemporaries of Columbus. By some process Columbus reasoned that the longitudes of [eastern Asia](/wiki/Eastern_Asia "Eastern Asia") and [Cipangu](/wiki/Cipangu "Cipangu") respectively were about 270 and 300 degrees east, or 90 and 60 degrees west of the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"). He said that he had sailed 1100 leagues from the Canaries when he found [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") in 1492. This was approximately where he thought the coast of eastern Asia would be found. On this basis of calculation he identified [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola") with Cipangu, which he had expected to find on the outward voyage at a distance of about 700 leagues from the Canaries. His later voyages resulted in further exploration of Cuba and in the discovery of [South](/wiki/South_America "South America") and [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"). At first South America, the *Mundus Novus* ([New World](/wiki/New_World "New World")) was considered to be a great island of continental proportions; but as a result of his [fourth voyage](/wiki/Fourth_voyage_of_Christopher_Columbus "Fourth voyage of Christopher Columbus"), it was apparently considered to be identical with the great Upper India peninsula ([India Superior](/wiki/India_Superior "India Superior")) represented by Behaim – the Cape of Cattigara. This seems to be the best interpretation of the sketch map made by Alessandro Zorzi on the advice of [Bartholomew Columbus](/wiki/Bartholomew_Columbus "Bartholomew Columbus") (Christopher's brother) around 1506, which bears an inscription saying that according to the ancient geographer Marinus of Tyre and Christopher Columbus the distance from [Cape St Vincent](/wiki/Cape_St_Vincent "Cape St Vincent") on the coast of Portugal to Cattigara on the peninsula of India Superior was 225 degrees, while according to Ptolemy the same distance was 180 degrees.[[65]](#cite_note-67)

### Early modern Ottoman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Early modern Ottoman Empire")]

Prior to the 16th century, knowledge of geography in the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") was limited in scope, with almost no access to the works of earlier Islamic scholars that superseded Ptolemy. His *Geography* would again be translated and updated with commentary into Arabic under [Mehmed II](/wiki/Mehmed_the_Conqueror "Mehmed the Conqueror"), who commissioned works from Byzantine scholar [George Amiroutzes](/wiki/George_Amiroutzes "George Amiroutzes") in 1465 and the Florentine humanist [Francesco Berlinghieri](/wiki/Francesco_Berlinghieri "Francesco Berlinghieri") in 1481.[[66]](#cite_note-68)[[67]](#cite_note-69)

## Longitudes error and Earth size

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_(Ptolemy)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Longitudes error and Earth size")]

There are two related errors:[[68]](#cite_note-russo-70)

* Considering a sample of 80 cities amongst the 6345 listed by Ptolemy, those that are both identifiable and for which we can expect a better distance measurement since they were well known, there is a systematic overestimation of the longitude by a factor 1.428 with a high confidence (coefficient of determination r² = 0.9935). This error produces evident deformations in Ptolemy's world map most apparent for example in the profile of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), which is markedly stretched horizontally.
* Ptolemy accepted that the known [Ecumene](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene") spanned 180° of longitude, but instead of accepting [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes")'s estimate for the circumference of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") of 252,000 [stadia](/wiki/Stadion_(unit) "Stadion (unit)"), he shrinks it to 180,000 stadia, with a factor of 1.4 between the two figures.

This suggests Ptolemy rescaled his longitude data to fit with a figure of 180,000 stadia for the circumference of the Earth, which he described as a "general consensus".[[68]](#cite_note-russo-70) Ptolemy rescaled experimentally obtained data in many of his works on geography, astrology, music, and optics.
